meta.title: W212 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes W212 engine — OM651, OM642, M271, M274, M272, M276, M156, M157. Real UK costs, the 2013 facelift changes, and honest reliability data. Get free quotes from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes E‑Class W212 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned W212 engine typically costs £1,500–£12,000 supply only, depending on engine — the volume OM651 diesel sits at £1,800–£3,800, while the M157 AMG V8 ranges up to £12,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,500 depending on the variant. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes W212 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM651 four‑cylinder diesel (E 220 CDI / E 250 CDI) — proven across millions of miles in taxi and fleet service, with genuine 200,000+ mile potential. The M276 V6 (E 350, 2012–2016) is the most reliable petrol option, free of the M272's balance shaft issues.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes E‑Class W212?

acceptedAnswer.text: Balance shaft sprocket wear on the M272 V6 (2009–2011 petrol models), timing chain stretch and injector seal failure on the OM651 four‑cylinder diesel, oil cooler seal leaks on the OM642 V6 diesel, camshaft adjuster wear on the M271 four‑cylinder petrol, and head bolt/camshaft adjuster issues on the M156 AMG V8.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes E‑Class W212 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends heavily on which engine and maintenance history. The OM651 diesel and M276 V6 petrol are the two most reliable options. The M272 V6 carries a known balance shaft risk on early models — check history before buying. The M156 AMG V8 is a masterpiece but demands meticulous maintenance and carries high repair costs.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes W212 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but a high‑mileage M272 V6 (E 300 / E 350, 2009–2011) without documented balance shaft history is the one purchase we consistently advise against. A neglected M156 (E 63 AMG, 2009–2011) with ticking noises is the second. An OM651 diesel with visible timing chain rattle should also be approached with caution.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my W212 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Check engine light with camshaft position codes (M272), timing chain rattle on cold start (OM651/OM642), oil leaks from the engine bay (OM642), ticking noise on cold start (M156/M271), or coolant loss (M156 head bolts) are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes E‑Class W212 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well‑kept £6,000 W212, a £3,500 OM651 recon is 58% of the car's value — borderline but viable. Against a tired £4,000 W212, the same recon is over 85% — walk away. Our 70% Mercedes threshold is the line. For a well‑maintained E 63 AMG with values above £15,000, a £10,000 M156 recon may still make sense.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes W212 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM651 diesel can exceed 200,000 miles with proper servicing. The OM642 V6 diesel is similarly durable. The M276 V6 petrol is capable of high mileage with regular maintenance. The M272's limiting factor is the balance shaft — addressed proactively, it can reach 150,000+ miles. The M156's head bolts and camshaft adjusters are the limiting factors — repaired, it's a durable engine but costs escalate.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the W212, S212 and C207/A207?

acceptedAnswer.text: The W212 is the 4‑door saloon (this page, 2009–2016), the S212 is the 5‑door estate (2009–2016), and the C207/A207 are the coupé and convertible (2010–2017) — which are based on the W204 C‑Class platform and are covered on their own Generation Pages. All share most engines but body‑specific labour costs vary.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes W212 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate, in the vehicle documentation, and on the engine block itself. The 8th character of the VIN often indicates the engine variant. Tell us your variant and approximate registration date (pre‑ or post‑2013 facelift) and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Mercedes E‑Class W212 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the fourth‑generation E‑Class — from the M271 and M274 turbo four‑cylinders to the V6 and V8 petrol range, the OM651 four‑cylinder diesel family and the OM642 V6 diesel. Real UK costs, honest failure data, and the 2013 facelift that brought new engines and improved efficiency. Backed by 1,250+ real UK enquiries in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes E‑Class W212 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the W212/S212 specifically — including the pre‑ and post‑facelift engine changes that define this generation's ownership story.

overview.intro: The W212 is the fourth‑generation E‑Class, launched in March 2009 as the successor to the W211. It introduced a more angular design language with quad‑headlights replaced by single‑lamp units, a longer wheelbase, and a range of new engines including the OM651 four‑cylinder diesel family. A major 2013 facelift brought significant styling changes, improved engines, and new safety features — reported to be the most significant mid‑cycle refresh Mercedes had ever undertaken. The W212 was succeeded by the W213 in 2016.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: W212 (Mercedes‑Benz fourth‑generation E‑Class platform) • Predecessor: W211 (third‑generation E‑Class) • Successor: W213 (fifth‑generation E‑Class) • Sibling body styles in this generation: S212 (estate), C207/A207 (coupé/convertible — based on W204 C‑Class platform)
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The W212 E‑Class generated significant UK engine enquiry demand in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED], concentrated on the OM651 diesel and M271/M274 petrol variants as these cars enter their second decade of service.

items.quote: “The 2.1‑litre four‑cylinder diesel is the W212's most dependable engine — proven across millions of miles in taxi and fleet service, with genuine 200,000+ mile potential. The 170 PS version delivers effortless performance without the V6 diesel's higher running costs.” Who it’s for: Buyers who want diesel economy and six‑figure mileage potential.

“The 2.0‑litre turbo four is a significant improvement over the M271 — smoother, more efficient, and free of the older engine's camshaft adjuster issues. 211 PS in the E 250 is plenty for executive duties.” Who it’s for: Buyers who want a modern petrol E‑Class without V6 running costs.

“The M272's balance shaft sprocket wear is this generation's defining risk — a design flaw that can lead to catastrophic engine failure. Mercedes acknowledged the issue but didn't extend the warranty as widely as with later engines.” Who it’s for: Buyers who understand the risk and have documented balance shaft history.

“The 6.2‑litre naturally aspirated V8 is a masterpiece — but punishing to repair. Head bolt failure, camshaft adjuster wear, and tappet issues are well‑documented. A full replacement runs well into five figures.” Who it’s for: AMG enthusiasts with deep pockets and full service history.

“The entry‑level diesel shares the same robust OM651 architecture as the more powerful versions — but costs less to buy and less to replace. Timing chain and injector seal maintenance are the only real demands.” Who it’s for: Buyers who want diesel economy on a tighter budget.

“Often overshadowed by the V8 models, the M276 V6 is actually the most refined petrol in the range — smoother than the M272, more reliable than the M156, and with 306 PS in the E 350, genuinely quick. It’s the sweet spot of the W212 petrol range.” Who it’s for: Buyers who want V6 refinement without AMG running costs.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Risk vs Reward — budget for balance shaft

economicsRule.title: The W212 rule
economicsRule.text: A £3,500 OM651 recon against a £6,000 W212 sits at 58% — that brushes our 70% Mercedes threshold. Against a tired £4,000 W212, the same recon is over 85% — walk away. A £4,500 M276 recon against a well‑kept £10,000 E 350 is 45% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Value the car first.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W212 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: OM651 (E 220 CDI) — cheap timing chain and injector seal maintenance, proven four‑cylinder diesel.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W212 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: M156 (E 63 AMG, 2009–2011) — head bolt failure, camshaft adjuster wear, and V8 complexity add up fast.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W212 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: M276 (E 350, 2012–2016) — V6 refinement without the M272's balance shaft anxiety or the AMG's running costs.

problems.title: M272 Balance Shaft Sprocket Wear (E 300 / E 350 / E 350 CGI, 2009–2011)
problems.rootCause: The M272 V6's balance shaft sprocket has a design flaw — the sprocket wears prematurely, causing timing chain slack, camshaft position sensor codes, and eventual engine failure if ignored. Mercedes acknowledged the issue but did not issue a recall.
problems.recommendation: If your W212 E 350 develops a check engine light with camshaft position codes, get the balance shaft inspected immediately — a £1,500–£3,500 repair is dramatically cheaper than the £3,000–£5,500 replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare M272 engine prices for your W212 E 350 →

problems.title: OM651 Timing Chain & Injector Seal Issues
problems.rootCause: The OM651 four‑cylinder diesel has two known issues — timing chain stretch (particularly on earlier examples) and injector seal failure (carbon buildup around injectors, causing the classic 'diesel knock' and loss of power).
problems.recommendation: If your W212 diesel develops a rattle on cold start or a tapping noise from the injector area, get it inspected immediately — a £400–£1,200 repair is far cheaper than the £1,800–£3,800 replacement that follows a failed chain or seized injector.

cta.label: Compare OM651 engine prices for your W212 E 220 CDI →

problems.title: M156 Head Bolt Failure & Camshaft Adjuster Wear (E 63 AMG, 2009–2011)
problems.rootCause: The M156 V8 has two well‑documented issues — head bolt failure (the bolts can stretch or break, leading to coolant leaks and potential engine damage) and camshaft adjuster wear (causing a ticking noise on cold start and reduced performance).
problems.recommendation: If your W212 E 63 AMG develops a ticking noise on cold start or coolant loss, get the camshaft adjusters and head bolts inspected immediately — a £1,500–£3,500 repair is dramatically cheaper than the £5,500–£10,000 replacement that follows a failed head gasket or seized camshaft.

cta.label: Compare M156 engine prices for your W212 E 63 AMG →

problems.title: OM642 Oil Cooler Seal Leaks (E 300 CDI / E 350 CDI)
problems.rootCause: The OM642 V6 diesel's oil cooler seals degrade over time, causing oil leaks into the 'V' of the engine. This can lead to oil contamination of the engine bay, belt failure, and eventually engine damage if ignored.
problems.recommendation: If your W212 diesel develops an oil smell or visible oil in the engine bay, get the oil cooler seals inspected immediately — a £600–£1,500 repair is far cheaper than the £2,500–£5,000 replacement that follows a neglected leak.

cta.label: Compare OM642 engine prices for your W212 E 350 CDI →

3. OM642 oil cooler seal leaks (V6 diesel)
